steps.text: The total mass of $r$ objects is $t$ kg, so the mass per object is $\frac{t}{r}$ kg.
The total mass of $146r$ objects is $\frac{t}{r} \times 146r = 146t$ kg.

choices.explanation: • $146 - t$ is a difference, not a product. Mass scales multiplicatively with quantity, so subtraction makes no sense here. Not correct.
• $146 + t$ is a sum. Same reasoning — mass must be multiplied, not added. Not correct.
• $\frac{t}{146}$ would be the total mass of $\frac{r}{146}$ objects, not $146r$. Division goes the wrong direction. Not correct.
• $146t$ equals $\frac{t}{r} \times 146r$, which correctly scales the original total mass by 146. That matches choice D — D is correct.

steps.title: Use the source rationale
steps.content: Choice D is correct. It's given that the total mass, in kilograms, of $r$ identical objects is $t$. To obtain the expression $146r$, $r$ is multiplied by $146$. Therefore, to find the total mass, in kilograms, of $146r$ of these objects, $t$ must also be multiplied by $146$. The result of multiplying $t$ by $146$ is the expression $146t$. Therefore, the total mass, in kilograms, of $146r$ of these objects, is $146t$.

steps.title: Check the choices
steps.content: Choice A is incorrect and may result from conceptual errors.

steps.title: Check the choices
steps.content: Choice B is incorrect and may result from conceptual errors.

steps.title: Check the choices
steps.content: Choice C is incorrect and may result from conceptual errors.
